input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10‑scale autonomous, teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology; its main physical twin components are the lower and upper chassis sections and embedded autonomy elements; it operates in a vehicular driving domain with ACC functionality and is suitable for digital twinning because it mimics resource‑constrained ECU constraints while enabling real‑time monitoring, computation offloading, and remote management.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface that displays real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports; (2) Computation Offloading – offloads the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT, whereby the DT computes the desired speed based on lead‑vehicle distance and returns the command to the vehicle; (3) Remote Management – enables operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands while viewing real‑time state data; these services target operators and developers and support monitoring, control, and remote operation of the ADAS system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system aligns all digital twin entities to a common time base with a synchronization accuracy requirement of 100 ms. Components exchange timestamps via the underlying ROS 2 middleware. No specific synchronization frequency is declared, but every update cycle that drives the physical twin must respect the 100 ms latency bound imposed by the hardware limits of safety‑critical vehicular applications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, organizing software into three logical entities—Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User. Each functional element (FE) is implemented as a ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. The Physical Twin Entity hosts resource‑specific services such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking; the Digital Twin Entity manages data processing, storage, and control logic; and the User Entity exposes the 3‑D visualization and control‑panel interfaces. This entity‑centric design enables clear ownership of functionality, facilitates deployment across heterogeneous hardware, and supports orchestration via standard ROS 2 topics and services.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from physical sensors flows into the Digital Twin via the Data Translation component, which serializes raw data streams, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every sensor input. LiDAR raw point cloud data, represented as individual points on a 2D plane, is captured by the vehicle’s LiDAR sensor and forwarded to the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking SW component. This component preprocesses the point cloud, generating tracked object representations and a projected driving path. Vehicle state, motor actuation, and steering servo state information are also transmitted. Inter‑component communication occurs over a ROS2 network (DDS over UDP), while inter‑entity data transfer uses an over‑the‑air Wi‑Fi network running UDP. Data is streamed in real‑time, and the architecture is designed for low‑latency visualization on a 3D Virtual Cockpit (Fig. 3a).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin employs a ROS2-based middleware for intra‑software component communication, leveraging DDS over UDP for message passing. Inter‑entity communication between the Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity occurs over a Wi‑Fi network utilizing UDP. The architecture emphasizes a real‑time data pipeline, with the Data Translation component ensuring consistent data formats and units across all streams. No explicit bandwidth, latency, or reliability metrics are documented, but the design implies a requirement for continuous, low‑latency UDP transmission to support live visualization and control.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The ISO 23247 standard defines a reference architecture (RA) for digital twin systems that organizes the architecture into distinct entities: a Data Collection and Device Control Entity, a Digital Twin Entity, and a User Entity. Each entity aggregates Functional Entities (FEs) responsible for discrete tasks such as data acquisition, device actuation, data transformation into human‑interpretable formats, and user interaction. The modular RA supports incremental evolution by allowing individual entities or FEs to be updated independently, but the standard itself does not prescribe explicit version‑control mechanisms or evolution‑management strategies.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The digital twin system complies with ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing, adopting its entity‑based reference architecture that defines separate entities for data collection, digital representation, and user interaction. The standard further permits the use of OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for implementing functional entities, thereby ensuring interoperability with standardized industrial automation protocols.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Security and safety provisions for the digital twin adhere to ISO 26262 functional safety guidelines. The safety lifecycle executes hazard analysis and risk assessment, deriving safety goals that map to safety classes A–D. Safety integrity levels (SIL) are specified and implemented through redundant sensing, fault‑tolerant software architecture, and fail‑safe mechanisms. Verification and validation of safety functions rely on simulation, fault injection, and formal proof aligned with ISO 26262 Part 6. The system also employs 3GPP TS 22.185–derived V2X security measures: mutual authentication via asymmetric PKI, integrity protection with MAC, confidentiality with AES‑GCM, and message authentication to prevent spoofing. These protocols enforce access control, secure key management, and logging to meet the communication security requirements for vehicle‑to‑everything services.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_
block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. _A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_
